region_of_origin: Tamil Nadu, India; widely consumed in Singapore

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for curd rice or thayir sadam; mapped to closest parent 'rice dish' class (FOODON_03311069) as the closest defensible ancestor.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A00LC (Rice-based dishes) used as the closest defensible category; no specific code for curd rice exists in the FoodEx2 hierarchy.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for curd rice or thayir sadam as of 2026.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical single serving of Chennai Curd Rice is approximately 250 g, equivalent to one standard plate as commonly served in South Indian restaurants and home kitchens in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Curd Rice — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Curd Rice (Chennai style), CR_ID G00010519. All per-100g macros and micronutrients are taken from this record with confidence 0.95. Saturated fat is not separately reported in the HPB record; value estimated from ICMR-NIN Indian Food Composition Tables 2017 for plain yogurt and cooked white rice components.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Chennai Curd Rice in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 56 (medium) based on the analogous food 'white rice mixed with yogurt' from Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care 31:2281–2283). The yogurt component lowers the effective GI of plain white rice (GI ~72) to approximately 56 due to the acidic pH of yogurt slowing gastric emptying and reducing starch hydrolysis rate. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (56 × 25.19 × 2.5) / 100 = 35.3 g per 250 g serving.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 231 mg/100 g; partially offset by potassium 327 mg/100 g and calcium 54 mg/100 g
hypertension.guidance: Sodium at 231 mg/100 g is borderline for hypertension management; however, the favourable sodium-to-potassium ratio (0.71) and calcium content are consistent with DASH-diet principles, so moderate portions with reduced added salt during preparation are advisable for hypertensive individuals.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Carbohydrate 25.19 g/100 g from white rice; medium GI ~56 partially moderated by yogurt acidity
type2_diabetes.guidance: The white rice base contributes a significant glycaemic load (GL ~35 per 250 g serving); individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should limit portion size to 150 g, substitute brown rice to lower GI, and pair with additional protein or non-starchy vegetables to blunt postprandial glucose excursions.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat (~1.8 g/100 g), zero trans fat, cholesterol 0 mg/100 g, and beneficial potassium and calcium profile
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Chennai Curd Rice has a low saturated fat content, no trans fat, and zero cholesterol, making it a heart-friendly option; however, sodium at 231 mg/100 g should be monitored in individuals with heart failure or sodium-restricted CVD management plans.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Fermented yogurt supports gut microbiome immune regulation; spice tempering provides anti-inflammatory polyphenols
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Plain yogurt provides live cultures supporting gut-immune regulation relevant to RA; RA patients with nightshade sensitivity should request chili-free preparation, and substituting brown rice reduces the glycaemic inflammatory load.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Chennai Curd Rice (Tamil: தயிர் சாதம், Thayir Sadam) is a classic South Indian comfort dish originating from Tamil Nadu and widely consumed in Singapore's Indian community. It is prepared by mixing freshly cooked white rice with plain yogurt (curd) until smooth and creamy, then finishing with a tempering (tadka) of mustard seeds, curry leaves, green chili, and ginger fried briefly in oil. The result is a mildly tangy, cooling, and lightly spiced rice dish with a soft, slightly sticky texture. It is commonly served as a final course in a traditional South Indian meal to settle digestion, and is popular as a light lunch or dinner in Singapore hawker centres and home kitchens. Nutritionally, per 100 g it provides 164 kcal, 6.5 g protein, 4.21 g fat, 25.19 g carbohydrate, 3.7 g dietary fibre, 231 mg sodium, 327 mg potassium, and 54 mg calcium. The yogurt component lowers the effective glycaemic index of the dish to approximately 56 (medium GI) compared to plain white rice (GI ~72), due to the acidic pH slowing gastric emptying. Clinically, it is suitable for most adults in moderate portions; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should monitor portion size and consider brown rice substitution, while hypertensive individuals should request reduced salt during preparation. The probiotic content of the yogurt supports gut health and may benefit individuals with inflammatory conditions such as rheumatoid arthritis.
common_user_queries: Is curd rice good for diabetes?
Is curd rice suitable for hypertension?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (CR_ID G00010519) with confidence 0.95. The dish is a well-documented canonical South Indian preparation. Saturated fat estimated from ICMR-NIN component data. GI estimated from the closest analogous food in the University of Sydney GI Database with documented rationale.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, full vitamin panel, phosphorus, zinc, selenium, and moisture data for this specific Chennai Curd Rice preparation are not separately reported in the HPB Foodo database (G00010519) or ICMR-NIN Indian Food Composition Tables 2017 as of 2026; these fields are confirmed unavailable in both primary approved sources consulted. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 — confirmed zero per HPB source; curd rice prepared without egg or meat contains no dietary cholesterol. saturated_fat_g (1.8 g/100 g) is estimated from ICMR-NIN Indian Food Composition Tables 2017 for the yogurt and rice components in the absence of a direct HPB value. GI of 56 is an analogous estimate based on white rice mixed with yogurt from Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care); no direct GI measurement for Chennai Curd Rice exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. Input name contained emoji characters ('👱' and '💋') which have been stripped from the canonical food_name per Veritas naming rules. Input health_suitability entries referenced incorrect nutrient values (e.g., 'carbohydrate is 20g/100g with fibre only 0.5g/100g' and 'Sugar is low at 2.5g/100g') inconsistent with the HPB per_100g data (carbohydrate 25.19 g, fibre 3.7 g, sugar 0.21 g); corrected values used throughout this record.

improvement_actions_taken: Iteration 1: Stripped emoji characters from food_name (input had '👱' prefix and '💋' suffix). Added mandatory Singaporean multilingual names: Chinese simplified/traditional, Malay, Tamil, Hindi. Added saturated_fat_g estimated from ICMR-NIN component data. Added GI value (56, medium) as analogous estimate from Atkinson et al. 2008 with full documentation. Corrected health_suitability narrative errors from input. Added Cardiovascular Disease health context. Renamed 'diabetes' condition to 'type2_diabetes' with human_condition_name 'Type 2 Diabetes'. Populated ontology with defensible codes and gap notes. Built comprehensive aliases_by_language and verified consistency with flat aliases array.
Iteration 2: Documented all -1 sentinel fields in notes per g3 rule. Confirmed cholesterol_mg 0.0 justification. Ensured ontology match_type values were set.

Iteration 3 (this iteration): Fixed g3 failure — expanded notes to explicitly document every -1 sentinel field with reason (not reported in HPB Foodo G00010519 or ICMR-NIN 2017) and named the approved sources consulted that confirmed unavailability. Fixed g5 failure — changed foodon_match_type from 'broader' to 'closest_parent' and foodex2_match_type from 'broader' to 'closest_parent' to conform to the valid enum values ['closest_parent', 'exact', 'none'] required by the validator.
